perturbation.original_text: 1.1.4. “Confidential information” means any information, electronic data and/or documents relating to the Protectable Interests which are not readily available to a competitor of the parties in the ordinary and regular c ourse of business;
perturbation.changed_text: 1.1.4. “Confidential information” means only information explicitly marked as confidential in writing and acknowledged as such by both parties. Information not explicitly marked or acknowledged is not considered confidential under this agreement.
perturbation.explanation: The original definition of 'Confidential Information' is broad, encompassing any information relating to Protectable Interests not readily available to competitors. The modified definition narrows this scope significantly, requiring explicit written marking and acknowledgement for information to be considered confidential. This directly contradicts the original intent of protecting a wide range of information.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 3.1. In the intere st of the protection and maintenance of the Protectable Interests, each party undertakes that it shall not, at any time whatsoever and notwithstanding the termination of the business relationship and/or negotiations between the parties for whatsoever reaso n, without the prior written consent of the other party: 3.1.1. use, disclose or divulge, whether directly or indirectly, the Confidential Information to any person (whether in the other party’s employ or not) or to any person or entity which is not a party to t he agreement; 3.1.2. use, exploit, disclose, copy, divulge or in any other manner whatsoever apply the Confidential Information disclosed to it for any purpose for which it is disclosed and otherwise than in accordance with the provisions of this agreement; 3.1.3. derive any benefit, whether directly or indirectly, from the Confidential Information and, without limiting the generality of the aforegoing, be engaged, involved, concerned or interest, whether directly or indirectly, in the economic exploitation of t he Confidential Information.
perturbation.justification: The changed text significantly narrows the definition of 'Confidential Information' to only include information explicitly marked and acknowledged as such. This directly contradicts section 3.1, which broadly protects 'Confidential Information' (defined in the original, broader sense) from disclosure or use, irrespective of whether it is explicitly marked or acknowledged. The new definition makes it substantially more difficult to enforce the protections outlined in 3.1, as information not explicitly marked, even if related to protectable interests, would not be considered confidential under the modified definition.

perturbation.original_text: 1.1.6. “the Protectable Interests” means collectively each party’s goodwill, its trade secrets, its t rade connections, its confidential information and its knowledge and know -how including (without limiting the generality of the aforegoing) information, documents and/or data relating to: 1.1.6.1. the names, contact details, product ranges, pricing and discounts o f any of the suppliers of products and services of the Business; 1.1.6.2. the names, contract details, product or service specifications, particular requirements and preferences of existing or prospective clients, as well as any discounts given to clients; 1.1.6.3. agreem ents with clients, business associates, principals or suppliers of the Business and the terms thereof; 1.1.6.4. the business philosophy, methods, systems and techniques of the Business; 1.1.6.5. existing or planned distribution, marketing activities and sales strategies; 1.1.6.6. the financial structure, details, performance, accounting records and/or operating results of the Business; 1.1.6.7. technologies; technological know -how and information systems utilised by the Business; 1.1.6.8. product and service design and plans; 1.1.6.9. specifications incl uding product specifications, recipes, product formulations; manufacturing processes and/or techniques; 1.1.6.10. all knowledge obtained by way of research and development; 1.1.6.11. scientific, business or financial data, know -how, formulae, processes, designs, sketches, p hotographs, plans, drawings, specifications, sample reports , models, studies, findings, methodologies, know -how and networking techniques, inventions, designs; 1.1.6.12. costs of manufacturing plant, equipment, raw materials and other manufacturing consumables; 1.1.6.13. the strategic planning of the Business, its expansion programmes and its buying, marketing, financial and distribution strategies and policies; 1.1.6.14. business connections in the Republic of South Africa and overseas; 1.1.6.15. business acquisitions, disposal, opportunitie s and strategies; 1.1.6.16. staff lists, salary levels, employment agreements, training programmes and policies; CEE VEE TRANSPORT CONSULTANTS SA (PTY) LTD 1.1.6.17. the existence and content of the discussions between the parties, as well as any other agreement which may be concluded betwe en the parties pursuant to such discussions; and 1.1.6.18. All other information in whatsoever form communicated to or acquired by the receiving party during or arising from the discussions between the parties.
perturbation.changed_text: 1.1.6. “the Protectable Interests” means only tangible assets owned outright by the disclosing party, specifically excluding confidential information, trade secrets, and business strategies. This definition is limited to physical property and explicitly excludes intellectual property of any kind.
perturbation.explanation: The original definition of 'Protectable Interests' is comprehensive, covering goodwill, trade secrets, confidential information, and know-how. The modified definition restricts this to tangible assets only, expressly excluding intellectual property. This drastically reduces the scope of protection and contradicts clauses that rely on the original broad definition.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 2.3. The parties further acknowledg e that each party’s Protectable Interests are legitimate proprietary and commercial interests which each party is entitled to protect.
perturbation.justification: The original text defines 'Protectable Interests' broadly to include things like goodwill, trade secrets, and confidential information. The changed text narrows this definition to 'only tangible assets' and explicitly excludes intellectual property. This creates a direct conflict with section 2.3, which acknowledges that each party's 'Protectable Interests' are legitimate proprietary and commercial interests that they are entitled to protect. The changed definition removes intellectual property from the scope of 'Protectable Interests', directly contradicting the intent and scope of protection outlined in section 2.3. This meets the definition of a Structural Flaw - In Text Contradiction, where the conflicting implications in the two sections creates uncertainty about the obligations and rights of each party.

perturbation.original_text: 2.2. In the course of the above interactions, the parties may disclose their Confidential Information to any unauthorised third party will cause considerable financial loss to the other party.
perturbation.changed_text: 2.2. In the course of the above interactions, the parties may disclose information, whether or not it is deemed confidential; such disclosure is at their own risk. The parties agree that they are not liable for any loss incurred due to the sharing of information
perturbation.explanation: The original statement asserts that unauthorized disclosure of confidential information will cause considerable financial loss. This establishes a basis for liability. The changed text directly contradicts this by stating that parties are not liable for any loss incurred due to sharing information. This creates uncertainty about whether a party can be held responsible for disclosing confidential information.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 3.3. The parties acknowledge that the unauthorized disclosure of the Confidential Information of the other party to a third party may cause irreparable loss, harm and damage to the party to whom the Confidential Information belongs and each party therefore indemnifies and holds the other party harmless against any loss, action, expense, claim, harm or damage, of whatever nature, sustained (whether directly or indirectly) by a party as a result of the party’s breach of its obligations in terms of this agreement.
perturbation.justification: The changed text states that parties are not liable for any loss incurred due to sharing information, even if it is confidential. This directly contradicts section 3.3, which states that parties are liable and must indemnify the other party for losses resulting from unauthorized disclosure of confidential information. The original text supported the contradicted text.

perturbation.original_text: 3.1. In the intere st of the protection and maintenance of the Protectable Interests, each party undertakes that it shall not, at any time whatsoever and notwithstanding the termination of the business relationship and/or negotiations between the parties for whatsoever reaso n, without the prior written consent of the other party:
perturbation.changed_text: 3.1. Either party can disclose information to third parties at any time. There are no restriction in place regarding the sharing of information. The need for consent is waived.
perturbation.explanation: The original clause establishes a strict non-disclosure obligation requiring prior written consent for any disclosure of Confidential Information. This clause is now contradicted. The modified clause states that either party can disclose to third parties without any restriction
perturbation.contradicted_text: 3.1.1. use, disclose or divulge, whether directly or indirectly, the Confidential Information to any person (whether in the other party’s employ or not) or to any person or entity which is not a party to t he agreement;
perturbation.justification: The original text (3.1) states that neither party can disclose Confidential Information without prior written consent. The changed text (3.1) explicitly states that either party can disclose information to third parties at any time, with no restrictions and waiving the need for consent. This directly contradicts section 3.1.1, which prohibits the disclosure of Confidential Information to any person not a party to the agreement, creating a strong in-text contradiction. Therefore, the answer is YES.

perturbation.original_text: 3.4. Unless the parties otherwise agree in writing, any documents or records relating to the Confidential Information of the other party which come into the possession of either of them during the existence of this agreement or at any time thereafter:
perturbation.changed_text: 3.4. All documents or records relating to the Confidential Information of the other party can be copied, stored, and used at will without agreement between the parties.
perturbation.explanation: The original clause places restrictions on the use of confidential information, requiring written agreement for anything other than specified usage. The modified text states that documents can be copied, stored, and used at will without any agreement.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 3.4.2. shall not be copied, reproduced, published or circulated by the party receiving the Confidential Information; and
perturbation.justification: The changed text in 3.4 allows copying, storing, and using confidential information at will without agreement, directly contradicting 3.4.2, which states that the party receiving confidential information shall not copy, reproduce, publish, or circulate it. The original text restricted usage unless otherwise agreed in writing, which is removed in the changed text. This creates a strong in-text contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 6.1. In the implementation of this agreement, the parties undertake to observe the utmost good faith and they warrant in their dealings with one anothe r that they shall neither do anything nor refrain from doing anything that might prejudice the rights, assets or interests of the other(s).
perturbation.changed_text: 6.1. In the implementation of this agreement, there is no expectation of good faith. The parties are only expected to abide by what is explicitly written in the agreement, irrespective of the effect on the other party.
perturbation.explanation: The original clause mandates that parties act in the utmost good faith and avoid actions that could prejudice the other party. The modified text removes the expectation of good faith, permitting actions that might harm the other party as long as they are not explicitly prohibited.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 6.2. Each of the parties undertake s to complete, sign and deliver all documents and to do all things within its powers t hat are necessary to implement the terms of this agreement.
perturbation.justification: The changed text in section 6.1 explicitly removes the expectation of good faith, directly contradicting section 6.2, which requires each party to undertake actions necessary to implement the terms of the agreement. By stating that there is no expectation of good faith and parties only need to abide by what is explicitly written, the modified text undermines the obligation to complete and sign documents and do all things necessary to implement the agreement, even if those actions are not explicitly stated, as parties are now permitted to act in self-interest regardless of the effect on the other party, as long as it isn't explicitly prohibited.
